name: Coll de Bòixols Loop
name_original: Ida y vuelta al Coll de Bòixols - desde Coll de Nargó
description: This difficult 53.2-mile road cycling route from Coll de Nargó to Coll de Bòixols offers significant climbs and incredible views in the Cata
region_intro: Road cycling around Coll De Nargó offers diverse landscapes within the Catalan pre-Pyrenees, characterized by mountainous terrain and river valleys. The region features a network of roads that traverse forests of oak and holm oak, with the Segre river flowing into the Oliana reservoir. This area provides varied road cycling routes, ranging from challenging long-distance loops with significant elevation gains to shorter rides. Best road cycling routes around Coll De Nargó The most popular road cycling route is Port del Comte and Coll de Port Loop, a 71.9 miles (115.7 km) trail that takes about 7 hours to complete. This challenging route features a significant elevation gain of over 2,700 meters and includes scenic sections like the Serpentinas de Raset. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Coll de Bòixols Loop, a difficult 53.2 miles (85.7 km) path. This route ascends to the Coll de Bòixols pass at 1,321 meters, offering expansive views across the landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Old Bridge – Coll de Llinars loop from Coll de Nargó, a 43.7 miles (70.4 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and offering over 1,300 meters of elevation gain, often completed in about 3 hours 55 minutes. Road cycling around Coll De Nargó is defined by its mountainous landscape, river valleys, and Romanesque heritage, with routes catering to different fitness levels. The network includes challenging climbs and varied terrain suitable for road bikes. The routes in Coll De Nargó are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Coll De Nargó's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Coll de Nargó?
A: Coll de Nargó is situated in the Catalan pre-Pyrenees, offering a diverse and mountainous landscape. You'll encounter roads that traverse river valleys, forests of oak and holm oak, and significant elevation changes. The region is known for its challenging climbs and varied terrain, making it a training ground for professional cyclists.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs in Coll de Nargó?
A: Yes, Coll de Nargó is well-known for its challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e943374449/port-del-comte-and-coll-de-port-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port del Comte and Coll de Port Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 115.7 km route with over 2,700 meters of elevation gain, featuring scenic sections like the Serpentinas de Raset. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e943404520/coll-de-boixols-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de Bòixols Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which ascends to 1,321 meters and offers expansive views.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks to look out for while cycling in the region?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty and unique landmarks. You can find stunning viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/153045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Rocas de Sant Honorat&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3037609" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Top of the Coscollet viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The area is also famous for its dinosaur egg fossil sites, with the Dinosfera Museum and Cretaceous Observation Centre nearby. Additionally, you might encounter Romanesque churches like Sant Climent, known for its lofty belfry.

Q: Are there any easier or family-friendly road cycling options around Coll de Nargó?
A: While many routes in Coll de Nargó are rated as difficult due to significant elevation gains, there are shorter loops available. For example, a version of the Coll de Bòixols loop starting from Coll de Nargó is 36.8 km with 904 meters of elevation gain, which might be more manageable for those seeking a less strenuous ride. However, dedicated 'easy' or 'family-friendly' road cycling routes are less common given the mountainous terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Coll de Nargó?
A: Given its location in the pre-Pyrenees, the best time for road cycling in Coll de Nargó is typically during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be hot, especially in the valleys, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential snow at higher elevations.

Q: Where can I find parking for my car and bike when starting a route in Coll de Nargó?
A: Coll de Nargó, as a base for many routes, typically offers parking facilities within the town or near popular starting points. While specific parking locations aren't detailed in the route information, it's common for villages in such regions to have public parking areas suitable for cyclists. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Coll de Nargó?
A: The road cycling routes around Coll de Nargó are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 9 reviews. Over 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the challenging climbs, diverse landscapes, and the quality of the roads suitable for road bikes.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the popular road cycling routes in Coll de Nargó are designed as loops. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e943374449/port-del-comte-and-coll-de-port-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port del Comte and Coll de Port Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e943404520/coll-de-boixols-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coll de Bòixols Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24551632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge – Coll de Llinars loop from Coll de Nargó&lt;/a&gt;. These circular routes allow you to experience the varied scenery without retracing your path.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with other activities or explore cultural heritage in Coll de Nargó?
A: Absolutely. Coll de Nargó is rich in both natural and cultural heritage. Beyond cycling, you can explore its significant Romanesque monuments, such as the Church of Sant Climent. The region is also famous for its dinosaur egg fossil sites, with the Dinosfera museum offering interactive exhibits. For nature lovers, there are opportunities for rock climbing and exploring ancient tombs.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Coll de Nargó?
A: There are 12 road cycling routes available around Coll de Nargó, catering to various levels of difficulty. Most of these routes are classified as difficult, offering significant challenges and rewards for experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints along the road cycling routes that offer panoramic views?
A: Yes, the mountainous terrain of Coll de Nargó provides numerous opportunities for breathtaking views. Cyclists often encounter stunning vistas from mountain passes like Coll de Port and Coll de Bòixols. Specific viewpoints to look out for include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/153045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Rocas de Sant Honorat&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3037609" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Top of the Coscollet viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, offering expansive panoramas of the surrounding landscape.

Q: What is the difficulty level of road cycling routes in Coll de Nargó?
A: The majority of road cycling routes in Coll de Nargó are considered difficult. Out of the 12 available routes, 9 are rated as difficult, with the remaining 3 being moderate. This indicates that the area is best suited for cyclists looking for challenging rides with substantial elevation gains.
tips: Great road and stunning views on the way reward for a doable, but not-so-easy climb.

Nice town located in the Pallars Jussá region. We can visit its castle, the Romanesque church or walk through the narrow streets. A magnificent opportunity to discover this small town is the day of the honey fair in Abella de la Conca.

Ideal place for cyclists. We can enjoy an incredible landscape and views throughout the climb to the pass. Located at a height of 1,321 metres, in the Catalan pre-Pyrenees, it joins the counties of Pallars Jussà and Alt Urgell.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this is a challenging road cycling route, rated as "difficult" with significant elevation gain. It requires very good fitness and is best suited for experienced cyclists due to its long, sustained climbs and steep gradients, some reaching peaks near 10%.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this round trip?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this round trip is approximately 5 hours, covering a distance of about 85.7 km. This duration is for cycling and does not include extended stops.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Coll de Nargó. While specific parking areas are not detailed, Coll de Nargó is a small town, and you should be able to find suitable parking for cyclists near the starting point.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be hot, and winter conditions, especially at higher elevations like Coll de Bóixols, can involve snow or ice, making it unsuitable for road cycling.

faqs.question: What scenic views or points of interest can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect incredible landscapes and views throughout the ride, especially during the climb to the passes. Notable points include Coll de Faidella (1250 m) and Coll de Bóixols, which offers cool views of the Catalan pre-Pyrenees. The route also passes through the settlement of Abella de la Conca.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on mostly well-paved surfaces. However, it features significant and sustained climbs, with average gradients around 6% and peaks near 10% closer to the summit of Coll de Bóixols. There are also undulating sections with smaller climbs and descents, particularly in the initial 3 kilometers from Coll de Nargó.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while there are no specific restrictions for dogs on the road itself, it's generally not suitable for cycling with dogs. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it's safely secured in a trailer designed for cycling, and always prioritize its safety and comfort.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road route in the Catalan pre-Pyrenees.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other paths. Notably, about 45% of the route is part of the [CIMA LL14] Boixols trail, and it also intersects with parts of the Montsec Bikepacking Loop 4 - Conca de Tremp (13%) and Volta a Gabarra (8%).

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through Abella de la Conca, which is a settlement, specific cafes or refueling points are not guaranteed. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks for the entire duration of this challenging ride, especially given its length and elevation.

faqs.question: What makes the Coll de Bòixols climb particularly challenging?
faqs.answer: The climb to Coll de Bóixols is known for its demanding nature, often rated as an HC (Hors Catégorie) climb. It features a long and sustained ascent of approximately 18.30 km with a total ascent of 951 meters and an average gradient of 5.20%, with sections reaching up to 10%. This makes it a true test of strength and endurance for cyclists.
